figure_1.Img_description: The image is a three-panel figure under the overall title "Conflict memory allocation: hyperparameter tuning." At the top of the page there is also a review watermark/header reading "Under review as a workshop paper at ICLR 2025." The left panel, titled "Training and validation loss," plots loss versus epoch. The x-axis is "Epoch" and the y-axis is "Loss." Multiple colored curves correspond to different training lengths (30, 60, 90, 120 epochs), and line style distinguishes training vs. validation loss. All curves drop steeply in the first several epochs from about 0.07/0.03 toward roughly 0.007-0.008, then flatten substantially; after the early phase, the train and validation curves appear close and mostly overlapping. The middle panel, titled "Final validation loss," plots final validation loss against training epochs (30, 60, 90, 120). A blue line connects the points. The lowest final validation loss is at 60 epochs, 90 is very close, 120 is somewhat higher, and 30 is the highest among the four. A red highlighted point and a vertical dashed line mark the selected 30-epoch setting. The right panel, titled "Throughput improvement," plots improvement (%) versus training epochs. A green line connects the points. The 30-epoch setting has by far the highest throughput improvement, around 7.1%, while 60, 90, and 120 epochs are much lower, around 1.6%, 0.5%, and 2.6%, respectively. A red highlighted point and dashed vertical line again mark the 30-epoch choice.
figure_1.Img_review: The figure communicates the intended negative result reasonably well: validation loss is best at a later epoch (60), but downstream throughput is best at an earlier checkpoint (30). The three-panel layout is sensible because it separates optimization behavior (left), predictor metric selection (middle), and downstream utility (right). However, there are several presentation issues. First, the left panel is visually crowded: multiple colors combined with train/validation line-style encoding are difficult to distinguish at this size. Second, the middle panel shows very small differences in validation loss on a narrow y-axis, but no error bars or seed variability; this makes the claim that 60 is definitively best somewhat fragile. Third, the throughput panel also lacks uncertainty estimates, which is important if the figure is meant to support a strong model-selection conclusion. Fourth, terminology is inconsistent: the figure title says "Conflict memory allocation," while the caption refers to a "congestion predictor," raising concern about whether the figure has been fully harmonized with the text. Improvements: unify terminology, enlarge or simplify the left panel, add uncertainty/error bars or confidence intervals to the middle and right panels, and state clearly whether points are single runs or averages over seeds/layouts/agent counts.
figure_1.Caption_review: The caption mostly matches the plotted trends: training/validation losses do flatten quickly, the lowest validation loss appears at 60 epochs, and the highlighted 30-epoch checkpoint is best by downstream throughput improvement (~7.1% vs. ~1.6% at 60). It also provides a concise and meaningful takeaway: predictor quality and scheduler utility are misaligned. That is a strong captioning choice. The main problem is inconsistency in naming: the caption refers to "the congestion predictor," but the figure title says "Conflict memory allocation: hyperparameter tuning." This should be resolved. The caption is somewhat long, but the length is justified because it explains the central negative result. If shortened, it should still preserve the key message that validation-optimal and utility-optimal checkpoints differ.
figure_1.Figrefs_review: The main-text references explain the figure well and arguably more completely than the caption. They clearly state the intended takeaway (hyperparameter selection is not utility aligned), summarize the smooth loss behavior, identify 60 epochs as best by validation loss, and quantify throughput improvements at 30/60/90/120 epochs. This gives the reader useful context and makes the figure's purpose clear. However, the text, like the figure, presents exact differences without any discussion of variance or statistical reliability. Also, the references assume that the throughput metric is averaged over layouts and agent counts, but the figure itself does not annotate that provenance. Overall, the figrefs are informative and well integrated, but they would be stronger if they acknowledged uncertainty and if the terminology were fully consistent with the figure title.

figure_2.Img_description: The figure is a 2x3 grid of line charts titled "Best model performance at 30 training epochs." Columns correspond to three layouts: Sparse warehouse, Bottleneck warehouse, and Dense obstacle warehouse. The top row plots throughput with y-axis label "Throughput (tasks per minute)," and the bottom row plots congestion with y-axis label appearing as "Congestion (events)." The x-axis is the number of agents (20, 30, 40, 50). Each subplot compares two methods: a gray dashed baseline labeled "Standard decoupled" and a blue solid method labeled "Conflict memory allocation," both with vertical error bars. In sparse throughput, both methods improve with agent count, with CMA modestly to clearly better from 30-50 agents and near-tied at 20. In bottleneck throughput, CMA is worse at 20, about tied at 30, and better at 40-50. In dense-obstacle throughput, CMA is much better at 30-40, tied at 20, and roughly tied again at 50. In sparse congestion, CMA is similar or slightly worse at low load but lower at 40-50. In bottleneck congestion, CMA is variable, somewhat worse at 20, similar/slightly better at 30, and lower at 40-50. In dense-obstacle congestion, CMA is lower at 20-40 and similar or slightly higher at 50, with large uncertainty.
figure_2.Img_review: The figure communicates the basic comparison effectively: rows separate metrics and columns separate layouts, making the experimental factors easy to parse. The use of error bars is important and appropriate because several differences are modest and uncertainty is non-negligible. The most visible result is indeed the large throughput gain for CMA in the dense-obstacle layout at 30-40 agents, with bottleneck gains appearing mainly at 40-50 agents. However, the figure also shows meaningful sparse-layout gains at higher load, which should not be ignored. There are several presentation issues. First, the image resolution is low and text is small, making exact values and labels difficult to read. Second, the legend is repeated in every subplot, which adds clutter; a shared legend would be cleaner. Third, panel labels such as (a)-(f) are missing, which would help cross-referencing in the caption and main text. Fourth, differing y-axis scales across panels are reasonable but can make visual comparisons harder and should be acknowledged. Finally, some error bars are large enough that claims about small differences, especially in low-load settings or dense 50-agent settings, should be stated cautiously.
figure_2.Caption_review: The caption is partially accurate but somewhat overgeneralized. The factual setup matches the figure: this is a best-model comparison at 30 training epochs, the top row shows throughput, the bottom row shows congestion events, and error bars summarize variation. The claim that CMA improves throughput most clearly in dense-obstacle layouts at 30-40 agents and in bottleneck layouts at 40-50 agents is supported. However, the caption underplays the sparse warehouse results, where throughput gains at 40-50 agents are also fairly clear. The statement that low-load settings are often worse is only partly supported: bottleneck at 20 agents is worse, but sparse and dense at 20 agents look roughly tied or only slightly different. The statement that 50-agent behavior is usually smaller or less consistent than medium-load gains is true mainly for the dense-obstacle layout, but less true for sparse and bottleneck throughput, where 50-agent gains remain visible. So the takeaway is directionally correct but too broad. The caption is a bit long and could be improved by being more precise and less sweeping, e.g., saying gains are most pronounced in dense 30-40 and bottleneck 40-50, while sparse gains are present but more modest.
figure_2.Figrefs_review: The main-text references do a good job of guiding the reader through the figure. They explain the purpose of the comparison, identify the key regimes where CMA helps, and provide approximate numeric examples, which makes the interpretation more concrete. The descriptions for bottleneck and dense-obstacle layouts align well with the figure. The sparse-layout discussion is also mostly aligned, though the text says CMA is slightly worse at 20 agents, while the plot appears closer to tied and may even suggest a very slight advantage depending on visual reading; this should be checked against the underlying values. The figrefs are stronger than the caption because they distinguish patterns by layout instead of compressing them into a broad summary. Overall, the figure is reasonably well integrated into the paper narrative, but the authors should ensure exact consistency between the textual claims and the plotted values, especially in the low-load sparse case.

figure_3.Img_description: The image shows a three-panel line figure titled "Mean throughput improvement versus training length" under the header "B Additional Sensitivity Plots." The panels are labeled "Sparse warehouse," "Bottleneck warehouse," and "Dense obstacle warehouse." In all three subplots, the x-axis is "Training epochs" and the y-axis is "Improvement (%)." Each subplot contains a single blue line with points at approximately 30, 60, 90, and 120 epochs, plus a dashed vertical line at 30 epochs. The sparse warehouse curve starts near +3% at 30 epochs, drops to around -1.5% at 60, rises to about +1.3% at 90, and ends slightly below 0% at 120. The bottleneck warehouse curve starts slightly negative at 30, rises to about +3% at 60, peaks near +6.8% at 90, and declines modestly to about +5.5% at 120. The dense obstacle warehouse curve starts very high near +19% at 30, drops to around +3% at 60, falls to roughly -7% at 90, and recovers to about +3% at 120. The trends are clearly environment-dependent and non-monotonic.
figure_3.Img_review: The figure is readable and the main trends are visible, but there are several issues. First, the y-axis scales differ across panels, which makes cross-layout comparisons of absolute improvement visually difficult and potentially misleading; a shared y-axis would improve comparability. Second, the figure reports means but provides no uncertainty visualization (error bars, confidence intervals, or shaded variance), which is a notable omission given that the values are said to be averaged over agent counts. Third, the dashed vertical line at 30 epochs is useful, but it would be clearer if directly annotated as the selected checkpoint. Fourth, the figure title overlaps with the caption semantically, and the appendix-style header inside the image is somewhat distracting. On the positive side, the panel titles and axis labels are clear, and the non-monotonic trends are easy to see. Overall, the figure conveys the intended qualitative message, but it lacks uncertainty information and could be improved for more rigorous comparison.
figure_3.Caption_review: The caption is largely accurate and consistent with the plotted trends. The statements that the dense-obstacle layout performs best at an early checkpoint and that bottleneck performance improves later are clearly supported by the figure. The broader claim that training length effects are environment dependent and non-monotonic is also justified. However, the caption does not mention that the sparse layout also appears to peak early and is strongly non-monotonic, so the summary is somewhat selective. The caption is informative and includes a concise takeaway, though it is a bit long. It would be stronger if it briefly summarized all three panels, clarified why the 30-epoch checkpoint was selected despite being suboptimal for bottleneck, and mentioned uncertainty or sample variation if available.
figure_3.Figrefs_review: No main-text references were provided (the figref list is empty), which is a significant integration problem. A figure like this should be referenced in the main text to explain its purpose, why training-length sensitivity matters, and how it justifies the chosen 30-epoch checkpoint. In particular, because the figure suggests that the selected checkpoint is a tradeoff across environments rather than universally optimal, the main text should explicitly discuss that rationale. As presented, the figure is insufficiently anchored in the paper narrative.

figure_4.Img_description: The image is a three-panel line chart with the overall title "Mean congestion prevention versus training length." The left panel is titled "Sparse warehouse," the middle panel "Bottleneck warehouse," and the right panel "Dense obstacle warehouse." In all three panels, the x-axis is labeled "Training epochs" and the y-axis is labeled "Prevention rate (%)." Each panel shows a single red line with circular markers at approximately 30, 60, 90, and 120 epochs, plus a dashed vertical line at 30 epochs. In the sparse warehouse panel, the values are roughly +2.5%, -15%, 0%, and -13%, showing strong non-monotonic behavior and mostly negative performance after 30 epochs. In the bottleneck warehouse panel, the values increase steadily from about +1% to +11%, +19%, and +27%, indicating monotonic improvement with training length. In the dense obstacle warehouse panel, the values are about +22%, -24%, -27%, and +18%, showing a sharp drop after 30 epochs and later recovery by 120 epochs. The figure therefore presents layout-specific trends in congestion prevention as training length changes.
figure_4.Img_review: The figure is readable and the three layouts are separated clearly, but several design choices weaken its scientific value. First, the y-axis scales appear different across panels, which makes cross-layout comparison of effect magnitude harder and can visually exaggerate or suppress differences. Since the figure compares the same metric across layouts, using a common y-scale would be preferable. Second, the figure reports means but provides no error bars, confidence intervals, or indication of variance across agent counts or random seeds, so it is impossible to assess statistical reliability. Third, the lines connect only four sampled checkpoints, which may imply a smooth temporal trajectory that is not justified, especially for the highly non-monotonic sparse and dense-obstacle curves. A horizontal zero baseline would also improve interpretability because the sign of the metric is central to the claim. Finally, the figure suggests an important scientific issue: the selected 30-epoch checkpoint is not consistently near-optimal across layouts, especially for the bottleneck layout where later training is much better. This deserves explicit discussion either in the figure or surrounding text.
figure_4.Caption_review: The caption is mostly accurate and matches the visible content well. It correctly states that the plot shows mean congestion prevention versus training length, averaged within each layout, and it usefully defines the metric and clarifies that negative values mean the method produces more congestion than the baseline. The statement about the dashed vertical line marking the selected 30-epoch checkpoint also matches the figure. However, the caption is somewhat purely descriptive and misses an important takeaway: performance improves steadily only in the bottleneck layout, whereas the sparse and dense-obstacle layouts show unstable or non-monotonic behavior. Adding such a short interpretive summary would make the figure more informative. There is also mild terminology inconsistency between the caption's precise definition (percentage reduction in occupancy-based congestion) and the y-axis label ("Prevention rate (%)"), which could be standardized.
figure_4.Figrefs_review: No main-text references are provided, so the figure currently lacks textual integration. This is a notable weakness. Because the figure appears to justify or contextualize the choice of a 30-epoch checkpoint, the main text should explicitly explain why that checkpoint was selected despite the fact that it is clearly far from the best value in the bottleneck layout and only mixed in the other layouts. The text should also discuss the strong non-monotonicity in two of the three panels and whether this reflects optimization instability, layout dependence, or averaging artifacts. As given, the absence of figure references means the reader is left without context, interpretation, or a clear takeaway.

figure_5.Img_description: The figure is a three-panel heatmap titled "Throughput improvement across training length and agent count." The panels are labeled "Sparse warehouse," "Bottleneck warehouse," and "Dense obstacle warehouse." In each panel, the x-axis is "Number of agents" with values 20, 30, 40, and 50, and the y-axis is "Training epochs" with values 30, 60, 90, and 120. Each heatmap cell contains a numeric annotation indicating the measured improvement, apparently in percent. The sparse panel shows mostly small-magnitude values near zero with mixed positive and negative signs. The bottleneck panel shows several moderate positive cells, especially around 30-40 agents and mid/high epochs, but still includes some negatives. The dense-obstacle panel shows the largest spread, with very large positive cells (roughly +40 to +45) and substantial negative cells (down to about -25). A colorbar labeled "Throughput improvement" is shown on the right, but it overlaps the dense-obstacle panel and obscures some of its cells.
figure_5.Img_review: The figure communicates the intended comparison across layouts, training duration, and agent count reasonably well, and the panel structure makes the cross-layout contrast easy to see. The qualitative trend is interpretable: sparse layouts have little effect, bottleneck layouts have moderate gains, and dense-obstacle layouts are high-variance with both large wins and large failures. However, there are several presentation issues. First, the colorbar is placed on top of the rightmost heatmap, obscuring parts of the dense-obstacle panel and making some annotations unreadable; this is a serious flaw. Second, the data include both positive and negative values, but the colormap appears sequential rather than diverging. A zero-centered diverging colormap would be much more appropriate for showing improvement versus degradation relative to baseline. Third, text and annotations are quite small, reducing readability. Fourth, if the values are percentages, the colorbar label should explicitly include "%" for consistency with the caption. Finally, because the interpretation hinges on sign and magnitude relative to zero, the visual design should make zero more salient.
figure_5.Caption_review: The caption is largely accurate and captures the main qualitative takeaway of the figure. The statements that sparse layouts stay near zero, bottleneck layouts show moderate positive regions, and dense-obstacle layouts have both the largest upside and the largest failures are consistent with the visible heatmaps. The caption also correctly explains that each cell corresponds to a layout/checkpoint/agent-count combination and represents throughput change relative to a baseline. That said, there are a few issues. The caption uses the term "checkpoint," while the y-axis is labeled "Training epochs"; the terminology should be aligned. Also, abbreviations such as "CMA" and the "standard decoupled baseline" may not be sufficiently self-contained unless they are defined very close by in the paper. The caption length is acceptable, and it includes a useful takeaway, but it could be slightly tightened while improving consistency with the axis labels.
figure_5.Figrefs_review: The provided main-text references are missing (shown as an empty list), so the figure currently lacks textual integration. This is a substantial issue: the paper should explicitly reference the figure and explain why it matters, what experimental dimension it is intended to support, and what conclusion the reader should draw. In particular, the text should discuss the strong dependence on layout type, the near-zero behavior in sparse settings, the selective moderate gains in bottleneck settings, and the high-variance risk/reward pattern in dense-obstacle settings. It would also help if the text clarified whether these are single-checkpoint results, averages over runs, or some other aggregation, and whether statistical uncertainty was assessed. As presented here, the missing figrefs weaken the scientific interpretability of the figure.

figure_6.Img_description: The image is a three-panel heatmap figure titled "Congestion prevention across training length and agent count." The panels are labeled "Sparse warehouse," "Bottleneck warehouse," and "Dense obstacle warehouse." In each panel, the x-axis is "Number of agents" with values 20, 30, 40, and 50, and the y-axis is "Training epochs" with values approximately 30, 60, 90, and 120. Each panel contains a 4x4 grid of annotated numeric values, representing percentage changes in a congestion-related metric. The color encoding ranges from dark purple for strongly negative values to yellow for strongly positive values. A colorbar labeled "Congestion reduction [%]" appears near the rightmost panel. The sparse layout shows mixed outcomes with several negative cells at lower agent counts and more positive cells at higher counts. The bottleneck and dense-obstacle layouts show stronger variability, with both large positive and large negative entries, indicating sensitivity to checkpoint and agent count.
figure_6.Img_review: The figure communicates the intended comparison structure reasonably well: three layouts, two experimental axes, and per-cell quantitative values. The panel titles and axis labels are clear enough, and the annotated cells are useful in principle. However, the presentation has several weaknesses. The most significant is the colorbar placement, which appears to overlap or intrude into the rightmost heatmap, reducing readability. The cell annotation text is quite small, making many values difficult to read. The color scale is also somewhat hard to interpret because the colorbar is narrow and crowded. If the figure's main point is checkpoint sensitivity, the design could do more to emphasize patterns across training epochs, for example with a better layout, clearer typography, or highlighting particularly unstable cells. It would also help to clarify whether values can legitimately exceed 100% and what exactly that means for the congestion metric. More broadly, if these are single-run checkpoint values, the lack of uncertainty visualization is a concern, since the figure may overstate a noisy effect. Suggested improvements: move the colorbar outside the panels, enlarge the figure and annotation text, ensure no overlap with data, clarify the metric definition, and consider adding variability/error information or aggregating across seeds.
figure_6.Caption_review: The caption is mostly faithful to the figure. It correctly describes the cells as percentage reductions in occupancy-based congestion relative to a standard decoupled baseline, and it explains that negative values mean worse congestion under CMA. The statement that the metric is highly checkpoint sensitive in the bottleneck and dense-obstacle layouts is consistent with the strong sign and magnitude changes visible in those panels. However, the caption could be improved. It does not explicitly mention that the figure contains three separate layouts, which is an important part of the figure structure. It also does not provide a concise overall takeaway beyond sensitivity; for example, it could note that improvements are inconsistent and can reverse sign across checkpoints and agent counts. The wording "training length" in the caption and "training epochs" on the axis are close but not identical; more precise terminology would help. Finally, if values over 100% are possible, the caption should briefly clarify how the percentage reduction is defined.
figure_6.Figrefs_review: No main-text figure references were provided, which is a significant weakness. Without figrefs, there is no evidence that the paper explains why these particular layouts, checkpoints, and agent counts were selected, or how the figure supports the paper's broader claims. A good textual reference should explicitly introduce Figure 6, state that it compares congestion reduction versus a decoupled baseline across three layouts, and interpret the main result: performance is highly sensitive to checkpoint in bottleneck and dense-obstacle settings, with mixed or inconsistent gains. As it stands, the figure appears under-integrated into the narrative.

figure_7.Img_description: The image is a three-panel comparative scatter/line plot titled "Tradeoff between throughput gain and congestion prevention." The left panel is labeled "Sparse warehouse," the middle panel "Bottleneck warehouse," and the right panel "Dense obstacle warehouse." In each subplot, the x-axis is "Throughput improvement (%)" and the y-axis is "Congestion prevention rate (%)." Four colored points represent agent counts of 20, 30, 40, and 50, as indicated by a legend titled "Agent count." Each point is also annotated with its numeric agent count, and the points are connected by a line to show progression with increasing agent count. In the sparse panel, the 20-agent point is around (-1%, -25%), the 30- and 40-agent points cluster around roughly (4%, 7-8%), and the 50-agent point is near (5%, 19%). In the bottleneck panel, the 20-agent point is near (-10%, -43%), the 30- and 40-agent points are near (0%, 16-17%), and the 50-agent point is near (5%, 28%). In the dense-obstacle panel, the 20-agent point is near (0%, 32%), the 30-agent point is near (40%, 28%), the 40-agent point is near (40%, 48%), and the 50-agent point is near (0%, -18%). The overall visual message is that the relation between the two metrics varies by layout and is not perfectly monotonic as agent count increases.
figure_7.Img_review: The figure communicates the intended two-metric comparison reasonably well, and the three-layout decomposition is sensible. It does show that performance changes substantially with agent count and that the relationship is non-monotonic in at least some cases. However, several presentation issues reduce clarity. First, the font size is very small, making axis labels, ticks, legends, and point annotations difficult to read. Second, the image resolution is low, so precise values are hard to judge. Third, the axes appear to use different ranges across panels, which makes cross-layout comparison less reliable; shared axis limits would strengthen the comparative claim. Fourth, the repeated legend in each panel is redundant and consumes valuable plotting area; a single global legend would be cleaner. Fifth, the dense-obstacle panel is visually the most complex, and the in-panel legend partially distracts from the data. More importantly, the figure is framed as a tradeoff plot, but both axes are "higher is better," so the plot is really a joint-performance comparison rather than a classical tradeoff unless the authors define why gains on one metric might come at the expense of the other. If tradeoff is the intended interpretation, the figure could be improved by highlighting Pareto-efficient points or explicitly marking medium-load regimes. Finally, the figure itself does not indicate the meaning of the "selected 30-epoch checkpoint," so the figure depends on external text for context.
figure_7.Caption_review: The caption is partly accurate and reasonably informative: it correctly explains that each point corresponds to one agent count in one layout and that connecting lines indicate progression from 20 to 50 agents. The statement that the relation is "not strictly monotone" is supported by the plotted trajectories, especially in the dense-obstacle panel and to a lesser extent in the sparse panel. However, the stronger claim that "Medium-load settings, especially in bottleneck and dense-obstacle layouts, usually offer the best joint tradeoff" is not fully supported by the visual evidence. In the dense-obstacle layout, 40 agents does indeed look like the strongest joint outcome, but in the bottleneck layout the 50-agent point appears better than the 30- and 40-agent points on both axes, and in the sparse layout 50 agents is also the upper-right point. Thus the caption overstates the medium-load conclusion, especially for bottleneck. The caption could be improved by softening that interpretation, explicitly defining what counts as "medium-load," and briefly clarifying why the 30-epoch checkpoint was chosen.
figure_7.Figrefs_review: No main-text references are provided here (the figref list is empty), which is a substantive issue. Without surrounding text, the figure lacks interpretive context: the reader is not told how throughput improvement and congestion prevention rate are defined, why the 30-epoch checkpoint is representative, or why these three layouts are the key test cases. The paper should integrate the figure more explicitly in the main text by describing the main qualitative pattern in each layout, justifying the checkpoint selection, and explaining what is meant by "tradeoff" and "medium-load." As it stands, the absent figrefs mean the figure is insufficiently anchored in the narrative and its conclusions are left under-explained.
